narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—and an impressive LSAT score of 43, which stands out well above the usual 36.70, your law school application showed real promise in several areas. You come from an upper-middle-class family background and plan to attend full-time, aligning with the vast majority of students in the dataset at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 77.4% probability that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales toward rejection to minimize the school's risk of low pass rates. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, placing you in the bottom 20% of your peers that year, far below the average decile of about 5.5. Next in impact was your Year 1 decile of 2—again, well under the typical 5.7 average—which signaled early academic struggles relative to others and reinforced concerns about consistency. While your strong LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success and offsetting some risks, it wasn't enough to counterbalance those lower decile rankings in the model's eyes. Compared to typical students who pass the bar on their first try, your high school performance deciles are notably weaker, even as your LSAT shines brighter than most. This combination made the model view your profile as higher-risk, despite your personal strengths. To move forward, focus on bolstering evidence of academic turnaround—perhaps through recent strong grades, additional test prep, or a post-baccalaureate program to lift those decile perceptions. Retaking or contextualizing your earlier rankings with explanations could help in reapplying. Many students in similar positions succeed by targeting schools with holistic reviews or gaining practical experience first. You're clearly capable, given your LSAT; with targeted improvements, a stronger application is within reach. Keep pushing—your background as an Asian male applicant brings resilience that's helped many overcome hurdles like this.
